notes: Direct ask with two specific time options; manager-adjacent peer wants confirmation before all-hands.
Generic newsletter digest; user has many of these.
Paying customer, explicit deadline (board review tomorrow), reproducible bug, clear ask.
Cold sales pitch. Could be archive if user wants the contact, but the right default for unrequested cold outreach is unsubscribe.
Says 'no action needed tonight' but the postmortem on Friday will need engagement; soft acknowledgement appropriate later.
Friend with a soft deadline (commit by Thursday). Short reply expected.
Phishing — deliberately do not engage. Archive (or report); definitely not reply or unsubscribe (unsubscribe link could itself be malicious).
Receipt — informational, no action.
Tech newsletter the user no longer reads.
Manager checking in with explicit ask for a 1:1; should respond same-day.
PR-merged notification; informational.
LinkedIn invitations are handled in LinkedIn, not email.
Speaking invitation with three-week deadline; not urgent today.
Family member asking about a weekend plan; reply same day.
Recruiter outreach. Could be unsubscribe (not interested), could be reply_later (polite no/maybe). Default is reply_later for any first-touch from a real person.
Auto-resolved alert; no action.
Customer integration question with a Friday ship deadline; concrete spec question we can answer.
Product marketing email.
Peer asking for a read on a design choice 'by next week.' Could be reply_now if user wants to be helpful immediately, but reply_later is correct given the explicit timeframe.
Itinerary confirmation — informational.
Vague 'do you have a sec?' Without context, most teams treat as reply_later. Could plausibly be reply_now if Chris is known to be a peer.
Daily news roundup the user no longer reads.
Beta user with a reproducible blocking crash; needs response and triage.
Investor with concrete date constraints (May 5-9). Time-sensitive scheduling.
Mass-market promotional spam.
Family group thread, photo share with no direct question. Could be archive but social norm is to acknowledge family — reply_later is safer.
Calendar reminder.
VP with explicit EOD deadline today.
Candidate follow-up; respond within a couple days but not necessarily today.
Critical security CVE on the API repo. Needs review and merge of the Dependabot PR.
Workspace digest the user doesn't read.
Mom with concrete decision and concrete options. Reply same day.
Invoice with a May 15 due date; needs forwarding to finance but not today.
Conference recordings notification — informational.
PM has a Friday lock for marketing calendar; concrete yes/no needed.
Cold but polite ask from a real person; could plausibly be archived if you triage hard, but the safer default for low-effort networking asks from real people is reply_later.
Social media digest.
Survey result needing analysis later this week; explicit 'no rush this week.'
Hard April 30 deadline with personal financial consequences if missed; also explicitly asks to confirm directly.
Newsletter the user wants to keep but isn't acting on now. Distinct from unsubscribe — the user reads it later.
Same-day lunch ask; reply within hours.
Product marketing email.
Sensitive heads-up. Reply_later is right because (a) responding in writing is unwise, (b) the source is anonymous; better handled offline. Could be archive if user wants to ignore.
Tax documents notification — informational, no action.
14-month paying customer with a detailed feature request and explicit asks (timeline). Acknowledge today.
FYI design doc with no direct ask. Could be reply_later if user is in the affected area, but the email itself says no specific ask, so archive is correct.
CEO-impersonation phishing. Archive/report; definitely do NOT draft a reply.
Newsletter.
Director with Thursday deadline and specific structured ask. Reply today.
One-word reply that closes a thread; no further response expected. Could be reply_later if user wants to send 'k' but the right default is leave it.
